are the unified [military forces](/wiki/Military "Military") responsible for the [defence](/wiki/Defense_(military) "Defense (military)") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), its [Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories") and the [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies"). They also promote the UK's wider interests, support international [peacekeeping](/wiki/Peacekeeping "Peacekeeping") efforts and provide [humanitarian aid](/wiki/Humanitarian_aid "Humanitarian aid").[[6]](#cite_note-7) The force is known as **His Majesty's Armed Forces** due to the  [British monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom")'s status as [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Head_of_the_Armed_Forces "Head of the Armed Forces") of the Armed Forces.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)

The **British Armed Forces** are the unified [military forces](/wiki/Military "Military") responsible for the [defence](/wiki/Defense_(military) "Defense (military)") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), its [Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories") and the [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies"). They also promote the UK's wider interests, support international [peacekeeping](/wiki/Peacekeeping "Peacekeeping") efforts and provide [humanitarian aid](/wiki/Humanitarian_aid "Humanitarian aid").[[6]](#cite_note-7) The force is known as **His Majesty's Armed Forces** due to the  [British monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom")'s status as [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Head_of_the_Armed_Forces "Head of the Armed Forces") of the Armed Forces.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)

Since the formation of the united [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") in  [1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") (later succeeded by the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") in [1801](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800"), and finally by the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Northern_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland") in [1927](/wiki/Royal_and_Parliamentary_Titles_Act_1927 "Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act 1927")),[[9]](#cite_note-Union1707-10) the British Armed Forces have seen action in most major wars involving the world's [great powers](/wiki/Great_power "Great power"), including the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War"), the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") and the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). Britain's victories in most of these wars allowed it to influence world events and establish itself as one of the world's leading military and [economic](/wiki/Economy "Economy") powers.[[10]](#cite_note-Johnston-11) The British Armed Forces consist of: the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), a [blue-water navy](/wiki/Blue-water_navy "Blue-water navy") with a fleet of 62 commissioned and active ships, together with the [Royal Marines](/wiki/Royal_Marines "Royal Marines"), a highly specialised amphibious light infantry force; the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"), the UK's [land warfare](/wiki/Land_warfare "Land warfare") branch; and the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force"), a technologically sophisticated [air force](/wiki/Air_force "Air force") with a diverse operational fleet consisting of both fixed-wing and rotary aircraft. The British Armed Forces include standing forces, the [Regular](/wiki/Regular_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Regular Reserve (United Kingdom)"), [Volunteer](/wiki/Volunteer_Reserves_(United_Kingdom) "Volunteer Reserves (United Kingdom)") and [Sponsored](/wiki/Sponsored_Reserves "Sponsored Reserves") Reserves.

[King Charles III](/wiki/King_Charles_III "King Charles III"), [sovereign of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Sovereign_of_the_United_Kingdom "Sovereign of the United Kingdom"), is the [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") and is styled as [Head of the Armed Forces](/wiki/Head_of_the_British_Armed_Forces "Head of the British Armed Forces"),[[11]](#cite_note-Forces_Queen_and_Armed_Forces-12)[[12]](#cite_note-army.mod.uk-13) with officers and personnel swearing [allegiance](/wiki/Allegiance "Allegiance") to him. Long-standing constitutional convention, however, has vested *de facto* executive authority, by the exercise of [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative_in_the_United_Kingdom "Royal prerogative in the United Kingdom"), in the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") and the [secretary of state for defence](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Defence "Secretary of State for Defence"). The Prime Minister (acting with the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom")) makes the key decisions on the use of the armed forces.[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) The [UK Parliament](/wiki/UK_Parliament "UK Parliament") approves the continued existence of the British Army by passing an [Armed Forces Act](/wiki/Armed_Forces_Act "Armed Forces Act") at least once every five years, as required by the [Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689"). Only a "standing army" requires reapproval by Parliament; the Royal Navy, Royal Air Force and the Royal Marines and any other forces are not included in the requirement. The armed forces are managed by the [Defence Council](/wiki/Defence_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Defence Council of the United Kingdom").

The [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") is one of five [recognised nuclear powers](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Non-Proliferation_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons"), a permanent member on the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council"), a founding and leading member of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") and party to the [AUKUS](/wiki/AUKUS "AUKUS") security pact and the [Five Power Defence Arrangements](/wiki/Five_Power_Defence_Arrangements "Five Power Defence Arrangements"). Overseas garrisons and training facilities are maintained at [Ascension Island](/wiki/Ascension_Island "Ascension Island"), [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain"), [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize"), [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda"), [British Indian Ocean Territory](/wiki/British_Indian_Ocean_Territory "British Indian Ocean Territory"), [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Akrotiri_and_Dhekelia "Akrotiri and Dhekelia"), the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), [Montserrat](/wiki/Montserrat "Montserrat"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[15]](#cite_note-europarl-16) The British Armed Forces provided military training to approximately 140 countries in 2024–25.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

### Organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Organisation")]

With the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"), the armed forces of England and Scotland were merged into the armed forces of the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain").[[9]](#cite_note-Union1707-10)

There were originally several naval and several military regular and reserve *forces*, although most of these were consolidated into the Royal Navy or the British Army during the 19th and 20th Centuries (the [Royal Naval Air Service](/wiki/Royal_Naval_Air_Service "Royal Naval Air Service") and [Flying Corps](/wiki/Royal_Flying_Corps "Royal Flying Corps") of the British Army, by contrast, were separated from their parent forces in 1918 and amalgamated to form a new force, the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force"), which would have complete responsibility for naval, military and strategic aviation until the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War")).

Naval forces included the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), the [Waterguard](/wiki/Waterguard "Waterguard"), later renamed the [HM Coastguard](/wiki/HM_Coastguard "HM Coastguard"), and [Sea Fencibles](/wiki/Sea_Fencibles "Sea Fencibles") and *River Fencibles* formed as and when required for the duration of emergencies. The [Merchant Navy](/wiki/Merchant_Navy "Merchant Navy") and offshore fishing boat crews were also important manpower reserves to the armed naval forces. Any seaman was liable to [impressment](/wiki/Impressment "Impressment"), with many so conscripted especially during the two decades of conflict from the French Revolution until the end of the Napoleonic Wars, and from 1835 registered on the *Register of Seamen* to identify them as a potential resource, and many of their seamen would serve part time in the [Royal Navy Reserve](/wiki/Royal_Navy_Reserve "Royal Navy Reserve"), created under the Naval Reserve Act 1859, and [Royal Naval Volunteer Reserve](/wiki/Royal_Naval_Volunteer_Reserve "Royal Naval Volunteer Reserve"), created in 1903.

The British military (those parts of the British Armed Forces tasked with land warfare, as opposed to the naval forces)[[17]](#cite_note-18) historically was divided into a number of [military forces](/wiki/Units_of_the_British_Army#Forces "Units of the British Army"), of which the British Army (also referred to historically as the 'Regular Army' and the 'Regular Force') was only one.[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) The oldest of these organisations was the [Militia Force](/wiki/Militia_(Great_Britain) "Militia (Great Britain)") (also referred to as the *Constitutional Force*),[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24) which (in the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England")) was originally the main military defensive force (there otherwise were originally only royal bodyguards, including the [Yeomen Warders](/wiki/Yeomen_Warders "Yeomen Warders") and the [Yeomen of the Guard](/wiki/Yeomen_of_the_Guard "Yeomen of the Guard"), with armies raised only temporarily for expeditions overseas), made up of civilians embodied for annual training or emergencies, and had used various schemes of compulsory service during different periods of its long existence.

The Militia was originally an all infantry force, organised at the city or county level, and members were not required to serve outside of their recruitment area, although the area within which militia units in Britain could be posted was increased to anywhere in the Britain during the 18th century, and [Militia coastal artillery](/wiki/Militia_Artillery_units_of_the_United_Kingdom_and_Colonies "Militia Artillery units of the United Kingdom and Colonies"), field artillery, and engineers units were introduced from the 1850s.[[24]](#cite_note-norman1987-25) The [Yeomanry](/wiki/Yeomanry "Yeomanry") was a mounted force that could be mobilised in times of war or emergency.[[25]](#cite_note-26) [Volunteer Force](/wiki/Volunteer_Force "Volunteer Force") units were also frequently raised during wartime, which did not rely on compulsory service and hence attracted recruits keen to avoid the Militia. These were seen as a useful way to add to military strength economically during wartime, but otherwise as a drain on the Militia and so were not normally maintained in peacetime, although in Bermuda prominent propertied men were still appointed *Captains of Forts*, taking charge of maintaining and commanding fortified [coastal artillery](/wiki/Coastal_artillery "Coastal artillery") batteries and manned by volunteers (reinforced in wartime by embodied militiamen), defending the colony's coast from the 17th century to the 19th century (when all of the batteries were taken over by the regular Royal Artillery).[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28) The militia system was extended to a number of English (subsequently *British*) colonies, beginning with [Virginia](/wiki/Colony_of_Virginia "Colony of Virginia") and [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda"). In some colonies, *Troops of Horse* or other mounted units similar to the Yeomanry were also created.[[28]](#cite_note-29) The militia and volunteer units of a colony were generally considered to be separate forces from the *Home* Militia Force and Volunteer Force in the United Kingdom, and from the militia forces and volunteer forces of other colonies. Where a colony had more than one militia or volunteer unit, they would be grouped as a militia or volunteer force for that colony, such as the Jamaica Volunteer Defence Force, which comprised the St. Andrew Rifle Corps, or Kingston Infantry Volunteers, the Jamaica Corps of Scouts, and the Jamaica Reserve Regiment,[[29]](#cite_note-30) but not the [Jamaica Militia Artillery](/wiki/Jamaica_Militia_Artillery "Jamaica Militia Artillery").[[30]](#cite_note-31) In smaller colonies with a single militia or volunteer unit, that single unit would still be considered to be listed within a force, or in some case might be named a force rather than a regiment or corps, such as is the case for the [Falkland Islands Defence Force](/wiki/Falkland_Islands_Defence_Force "Falkland Islands Defence Force") and the [Royal Montserrat Defence Force](/wiki/Royal_Montserrat_Defence_Force "Royal Montserrat Defence Force"). The militia, yeomanry and volunteer forces collectively were known as the *reserve forces*, *auxiliary forces*, or *local forces*. Officers of these forces could not sit on courts martial of regular forces personnel. The [Mutiny Act](/wiki/Mutiny_Act "Mutiny Act") did not apply to members of the Reserve Forces.

The other regular military force that existed alongside the British Army was the [Board of Ordnance](/wiki/Board_of_Ordnance "Board of Ordnance"), which included the *Ordnance Military Corps* (made up of the Royal Artillery, Royal Engineers, and the Royal Sappers and Miners), as well as the originally-civilian [Commissariat Stores](/wiki/Commissariat#19th_century "Commissariat") and transport departments, as well as barracks departments, ordnance factories and various other functions supporting the various naval and military forces.[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33) The English Army, subsequently the British Army once Scottish regiments were moved onto its establishment following the Union of the Kingdoms of Scotland and England, was originally a separate force from these, but absorbed the Ordnance Military Corps and various previously civilian departments after the Board of Ordnance was abolished in 1855.[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35) The *Reserve Forces* (which referred to the Home Yeomanry, Militia and Volunteer Forces before the 1859 creation of the [British Army *Regular Reserve*](/wiki/Regular_Reserve_(United_Kingdom)#Army_Reserve_(Regular) "Regular Reserve (United Kingdom)") by [Secretary of State for War](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_War "Secretary of State for War") [Sidney Herbert](/wiki/Sidney_Herbert,_1st_Baron_Herbert_of_Lea "Sidney Herbert, 1st Baron Herbert of Lea"), and re-organised under the [Reserve Force Act 1867](/wiki/Reserve_Force_Act_1867 "Reserve Force Act 1867"))[[35]](#cite_note-36) were increasingly integrated with the British Army through a succession of reforms over the last two decades of the 19th century (in 1871, command of the Auxiliary Forces in the British Isles was taken from the [Lords-Lieutenant of counties](/wiki/Lord-Lieutenant "Lord-Lieutenant") and transferred to the [War Office](/wiki/War_Office "War Office"), though colonial governors retained control of their militia and volunteer forces, and by the end of the century, at the latest, any unit wholly or partly funded from Army funds was considered part of the British Army) and the early years of the 20th century,[[36]](#cite_note-37) whereby the Reserve Forces units mostly lost their own identities and became numbered [Territorial Force](/wiki/Territorial_Force "Territorial Force") sub-units of regular British Army corps or regiments (the Home Militia had followed this path, with the Militia Infantry units becoming numbered battalions of British Army regiments, and the Militia Artillery integrating within Royal Artillery territorial divisions in 1882 and 1889, and becoming parts of the [Royal Field Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Field_Artillery "Royal Field Artillery") or [Royal Garrison Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Garrison_Artillery "Royal Garrison Artillery") in 1902 (though retaining their traditional corps names), but was not merged into the Territorial Force when it was created in 1908 (by the merger of the Yeomanry and Volunteer Force). The Militia was instead renamed the *Special Reserve*,[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40) and was permanently suspended after the First World War (although a handful of Militia units survived in the United Kingdom, its colonies, and the Crown Dependencies). Unlike the Home, Imperial Fortress and Crown Dependency Militia and Volunteer units and forces that continued to exist after the First World War, although parts of the British military, most were not considered parts of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army")[[40]](#cite_note-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42) unless they received Army funds, as was the case for the [Bermuda Militia Artillery](/wiki/Bermuda_Militia_Artillery "Bermuda Militia Artillery") and the [Bermuda Volunteer Rifle Corps](/wiki/Bermuda_Volunteer_Rifle_Corps "Bermuda Volunteer Rifle Corps"),[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[24]](#cite_note-norman1987-25) which was generally only the case for those in the Channel Islands or the Imperial Fortress colonies (Nova Scotia, before [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation"), Bermuda, Gibraltar, and Malta).[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46) Today, the British Army is the only Home British military force (unless the [Army Cadet Force](/wiki/Army_Cadet_Force "Army Cadet Force") and the [Combined Cadet Force](/wiki/Combined_Cadet_Force "Combined Cadet Force") are considered), including both the regular army and the forces it absorbed, though British military units organised on Territorial lines remain in British Overseas Territories that are still not considered formally part of the British Army, with only the [Royal Gibraltar Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Gibraltar_Regiment "Royal Gibraltar Regiment") and the [Royal Bermuda Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Bermuda_Regiment "Royal Bermuda Regiment") (an amalgam of the old Bermuda Militia Artillery and Bermuda Volunteer Rifle Corps) appearing on the British Army order of precedence and in the Army List.

Confusingly, and similarly to the dual meaning of the word [Corps](/wiki/Corps#United_Kingdom "Corps") in the British Army. As an example, the 1st Battalion of the [King's Royal Rifle Corps](/wiki/King%27s_Royal_Rifle_Corps "King's Royal Rifle Corps") was in 1914 part of the 6th Brigade that was part of the [2nd Infantry Division](/wiki/2nd_Infantry_Division_(United_Kingdom) "2nd Infantry Division (United Kingdom)"), which was itself part of [1st Army Corps](/wiki/I_Corps_(United_Kingdom) "I Corps (United Kingdom)")), the British Army sometimes also used the term [expeditionary force](/wiki/Expeditionary_warfare "Expeditionary warfare") or [field force](/wiki/Field_force "Field force") to describe a body made up of British Army units, most notably the [British Expeditionary Force](/wiki/British_Expeditionary_Force_(World_War_I) "British Expeditionary Force (World War I)"), or of a mixture of British Army, Indian Army, or Imperial auxiliary units, such as the [Malakand Field Force](/wiki/Siege_of_Malakand#Malakand_Field_Force "Siege of Malakand") (this is similarly to the naval use of the term [task force](/wiki/Task_force "Task force")). In this usage, *force* is used to describe a self-reliant body able to act without external support, at least within the parameters of the task or objective for which it is employed.

### British Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: British Empire")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/The_Siege_of_Gibraltar%2C_1782_by_George_Carter.jpg/250px-The_Siege_of_Gibraltar%2C_1782_by_George_Carter.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Siege_of_Gibraltar,_1782_by_George_Carter.jpg)

The [Great Siege of Gibraltar](/wiki/Great_Siege_of_Gibraltar "Great Siege of Gibraltar"), showing the defeat of the Franco-Spanish assault in September 1782

During the later half of the 17th century, and in particular, throughout the 18th century, British foreign policy sought to contain the expansion of rival European powers through military, diplomatic and commercial means, especially of its chief competitors [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), and [France](/wiki/France "France"). This saw Britain engage in a number of intense conflicts over colonial possessions and world trade, including a long string of Anglo-Spanish and Anglo-Dutch wars, as well as a series of "world wars" with France, such as; the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") (1756–1763), the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") (1792–1802) and the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") (1803–1815). During the Napoleonic wars, the Royal Navy victory at [Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar") (1805) under the command of [Horatio Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson,_1st_Viscount_Nelson "Horatio Nelson, 1st Viscount Nelson") (aboard [HMS *Victory*](/wiki/HMS_Victory "HMS Victory")) marked the culmination of British maritime supremacy, and left the Navy in a position of uncontested hegemony at sea.[[46]](#cite_note-47) By 1815 and the conclusion of the Napoleonic Wars, Britain had risen to become the world's dominant [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") and the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") subsequently presided over a period of relative peace, known as [Pax Britannica](/wiki/Pax_Britannica "Pax Britannica").[[10]](#cite_note-Johnston-11)[[47]](#cite_note-48)

With Britain's old rivals no-longer a threat, the 19th century saw the emergence of a new rival, the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), and a strategic competition in what became known as [The Great Game](/wiki/The_Great_Game "The Great Game") for supremacy in [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia").[[48]](#cite_note-keay419-49) Britain feared that Russian expansionism in the region would eventually threaten the [Empire](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") in India.[[48]](#cite_note-keay419-49) In response, Britain undertook a number of pre-emptive actions against perceived Russian ambitions, including the [First Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Afghan_War "First Anglo-Afghan War") (1839–1842), the [Second Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Afghan_War "Second Anglo-Afghan War") (1878–1880)[[49]](#cite_note-Schmidt-50) and the [British expedition to Tibet](/wiki/British_expedition_to_Tibet "British expedition to Tibet") (1903–1904). During this period, Britain also sought to maintain the [balance of power](/wiki/Balance_of_power_(international_relations) "Balance of power (international relations)") in Europe, particularly against Russian expansionism,[[50]](#cite_note-51) who at the expense of the waning [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") had ambitions to "carve up the European part of Turkey".[[51]](#cite_note-ALambert-52) This ultimately led to British involvement in the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War") (1854–1856) against the Russian Empire.[[51]](#cite_note-ALambert-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Royal_Irish_Rifles_ration_party_Somme_July_1916.jpg/250px-Royal_Irish_Rifles_ration_party_Somme_July_1916.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Irish_Rifles_ration_party_Somme_July_1916.jpg)

[Royal Irish Rifles](/wiki/Royal_Irish_Rifles "Royal Irish Rifles") soldiers at the [Battle of the Somme](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Somme "Battle of the Somme") in 1916

### First World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: First World War")]

The beginning of the 20th century served to [reduce tensions](/wiki/Anglo-Russian_Entente "Anglo-Russian Entente") between Britain and the Russian Empire, partly due to the emergence of a unified [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"). The era brought about an [Anglo-German naval arms race](/wiki/Anglo-German_naval_arms_race "Anglo-German naval arms race"), which encouraged significant advancements in maritime technology, including [Dreadnoughts](/wiki/Dreadnought "Dreadnought"), [torpedoes](/wiki/Torpedo "Torpedo"), [submarines](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine")), and, in 1906, Britain determined that its only likely naval enemy was Germany.[[52]](#cite_note-53) The [accumulated tensions](/wiki/Causes_of_World_War_I "Causes of World War I") in European relations finally broke out into the hostilities of the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") (1914–1918), in what is recognised today, as the most devastating war in British military history, with nearly 800,000 men killed and over 2 million wounded.[[53]](#cite_note-54) [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") victory resulted in the defeat of the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers"), the end of the German Empire, the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") and the establishment of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations").

### Second World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Second World War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Operation_Overlord_%28the_Normandy_Landings%29-_D-day_6_June_1944_B5098.jpg/250px-Operation_Overlord_%28the_Normandy_Landings%29-_D-day_6_June_1944_B5098.jpg)](/wiki/File:Operation_Overlord_(the_Normandy_Landings)-_D-day_6_June_1944_B5098.jpg)

Men of [No. 4 Commando](/wiki/No._4_Commando "No. 4 Commando") being briefed before the [Normandy landings](/wiki/Normandy_landings "Normandy landings") in 1944

Germany was defeated in the First World War, but by 1933 [fascism](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism") had given rise to [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), which under the leadership of [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") re-militarised in defiance of the Treaty of Versailles. Once again [tensions accumulated](/wiki/Causes_of_World_War_II "Causes of World War II") in European relations, and following Germany's [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland") in September 1939, the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") began (1939–1945).[[54]](#cite_note-55) The conflict was the most widespread in British history, with British Empire and [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") troops engaged in military campaigns in Europe, [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"), the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), and the [Far East](/wiki/Far_East "Far East"). Approximately 390,000 British Empire and Commonwealth troops died.[[55]](#cite_note-56) [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") victory resulted in the defeat of the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") and the establishment of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), replacing the League of nations.

### Cold War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cold War")]

Main article: [Outline of the British Armed Forces at the end of the Cold War](/wiki/Outline_of_the_British_Armed_Forces_at_the_end_of_the_Cold_War "Outline of the British Armed Forces at the end of the Cold War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Avro_Vulcan_Bomber_RAF.JPEG/250px-Avro_Vulcan_Bomber_RAF.JPEG)](/wiki/File:Avro_Vulcan_Bomber_RAF.JPEG)

The [Vulcan Bomber](/wiki/Vulcan_Bomber "Vulcan Bomber") was the mainstay of Britain's airborne nuclear capability for much of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War").

Post–Second World War economic and political decline, as well as changing attitudes in British society and government, were reflected by the armed forces' contracting global role,[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-Focus_on_Euope-58) and later epitomised by its political defeat during the [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis") (1956).[[58]](#cite_note-59) Reflecting [Britain's new role in the world](/wiki/British_Empire#Decolonisation_and_decline_(1945–1997) "British Empire") and the escalation of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") (1947–1991), the country became a founding member of the [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") military alliance in 1949. [Defence Reviews](/wiki/Defence_Review "Defence Review"), such as those in [1957](/wiki/1957_Defence_White_Paper "1957 Defence White Paper") and [1966](/wiki/1966_Defence_White_Paper "1966 Defence White Paper"), announced significant reductions in conventional forces,[[59]](#cite_note-60) the pursuement of a doctrine based on [nuclear deterrence](/wiki/Nuclear_deterrence "Nuclear deterrence"),[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62) and a permanent military withdrawal [east of Suez](/wiki/East_of_Suez "East of Suez").[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64) By the mid-1970s, the armed forces had reconfigured to focus on the responsibilities allocated to them by NATO.[[57]](#cite_note-Focus_on_Euope-58)[[64]](#cite_note-Navy-45-2000-65)[[65]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-66) The [British Army of the Rhine](/wiki/British_Army_of_the_Rhine "British Army of the Rhine") and [RAF Germany](/wiki/RAF_Germany "RAF Germany") consequently represented the largest and most important overseas commitments that the armed forces had during this period,[[66]](#cite_note-67) while the Royal Navy developed an [anti-submarine warfare](/wiki/Anti-submarine_warfare "Anti-submarine warfare") specialisation, with a particular focus on countering [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") submarines in the Eastern Atlantic and [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea").[[64]](#cite_note-Navy-45-2000-65)

While NATO obligations took increased prominence, Britain nonetheless found itself engaged in a number of low-intensity conflicts, including a spate of insurgencies against colonial occupation.[[67]](#cite_note-Insurgencies-68) However the [Dhofar Rebellion](/wiki/Dhofar_Rebellion "Dhofar Rebellion") (1962–1976) and [The Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles") (1969–1998) emerged as the primary operational concerns of the armed forces.[[67]](#cite_note-Insurgencies-68) Perhaps the most important conflict during the Cold War, at least in the context of British defence policy, was the [Falklands War](/wiki/Falklands_War "Falklands War") (1982).[[68]](#cite_note-Gibran-69)

Since [the end of the Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War_(1985%E2%80%9391) "Cold War (1985–91)"), an increasingly international role for the armed forces has been pursued, with re-structuring to deliver a greater focus on [expeditionary warfare](/wiki/Expeditionary_warfare "Expeditionary warfare") and [power projection](/wiki/Power_projection "Power projection").[[69]](#cite_note-Adrian-70) This entailed the armed forces often constituting a major component in [peacekeeping](/wiki/Peacekeeping "Peacekeeping") and humanitarian missions under the auspices of the United Nations, NATO, and other multinational operations,[[70]](#cite_note-71) including: peacekeeping responsibilities in the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") and [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"), the 2000 [intervention in Sierra Leone](/wiki/Operation_Palliser "Operation Palliser") and participation in the UN-mandated [no-fly zone over Libya](/wiki/Libyan_no-fly_zone "Libyan no-fly zone") (2011). Post-[9/11](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"), the armed forces became heavily committed to the [war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror") (2001–present), with lengthy campaigns in [Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)") (2001–2021) and [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War") (2003–2009), and more recently as part of the [Military intervention against ISIL](/wiki/Military_intervention_against_ISIL "Military intervention against ISIL") (2014–present). Britain's military intervention against [Islamic State](/wiki/Islamic_State_of_Iraq_and_the_Levant "Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant") was expanded following a parliamentary vote to launch a bombing campaign over [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"); an extension of the bombing campaign requested by the Iraqi government against the same group. In addition to the aerial campaign, the British Army has trained and supplied allies on the ground and the [Special Air Service](/wiki/Special_Air_Service "Special Air Service"), the [Special Boat Service](/wiki/Special_Boat_Service "Special Boat Service"), and the [Special Reconnaissance Regiment](/wiki/Special_Reconnaissance_Regiment "Special Reconnaissance Regiment") (British special forces) has carried out various missions on the ground in both Syria and Iraq.

The armed forces have also been called upon to assist with national emergencies through the provisions of the [military aid to the civil authorities](/wiki/Military_aid_to_the_civil_authorities "Military aid to the civil authorities") (MACA) mechanism. This has seen the armed forces assist government departments and civil authorities responding to flooding, food shortages, wildfires, terrorist attacks and the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom");[[71]](#cite_note-72) the armed forces' support to the latter falls under [Operation Rescript](/wiki/Operation_Rescript "Operation Rescript"), described as the UK's "biggest ever homeland military operation in peacetime" by the Ministry of Defence.[[72]](#cite_note-peacetime-73)

Figures released by the Ministry of Defence on 31 March 2016 show that 7,185 British Armed Forces personnel have lost their lives in [medal earning](/wiki/Military_awards_and_decorations_of_the_United_Kingdom "Military awards and decorations of the United Kingdom") theatres since the end of the Second World War.[[73]](#cite_note-Deaths-74)

In 2025, under Prime Minister Keir Starmer, the UK published a new [Strategic Defence Review](/wiki/Strategic_Defence_Review_(2025) "Strategic Defence Review (2025)") (SDR) calling for a shift to "warfighting readiness". Major investments were announced, including the expansion of the [SSN-AUKUS](/wiki/SSN-AUKUS "SSN-AUKUS") attack submarine program to up to 12 boats, acquisition of long-range weapons and advanced air-defence systems, and a possible entry into NATO's nuclear-sharing scheme via the F-35A platform.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

## Today

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Today")]

### Command

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Command")]

[![Elizabeth in red uniform on a black horse](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/ElizabethIItroopingcolour_crop.jpg/250px-ElizabethIItroopingcolour_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:ElizabethIItroopingcolour_crop.jpg)

Then Commander-in-Chief Queen Elizabeth II riding [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_(horse) "Burmese (horse)") at the 1986 [Trooping the Colour](/wiki/Trooping_the_Colour "Trooping the Colour") ceremony

See also: [UK parliamentary approval for military action](/wiki/UK_parliamentary_approval_for_military_action "UK parliamentary approval for military action")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Ministry_of_Defence_Main_Building_MOD_45150121.jpg/250px-Ministry_of_Defence_Main_Building_MOD_45150121.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ministry_of_Defence_Main_Building_MOD_45150121.jpg)

The [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)") building at [Whitehall](/wiki/Whitehall "Whitehall"), [Westminster](/wiki/City_of_Westminster "City of Westminster"), [London](/wiki/London "London")

[King Charles III](/wiki/King_Charles_III "King Charles III"), [sovereign of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Sovereign_of_the_United_Kingdom "Sovereign of the United Kingdom"), is the [Head of the Armed Forces](/wiki/Head_of_the_British_Armed_Forces "Head of the British Armed Forces"),[[11]](#cite_note-Forces_Queen_and_Armed_Forces-12)[[12]](#cite_note-army.mod.uk-13) with officers and personnel swearing [allegiance](/wiki/Allegiance "Allegiance") to him. Long-standing constitutional convention, however, has *de facto* vested military authority and associated [royal prerogative powers](/wiki/Royal_prerogative_in_the_United_Kingdom "Royal prerogative in the United Kingdom") in the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") and the [secretary of state for defence](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Defence "Secretary of State for Defence"), with the former (acting with the support of the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom")) making the key decisions on the use of the armed forces. As the prime minister is not the formal head of the armed forces, the chief of the defence staff could refuse a direction by them to use the [UK's nuclear arsenal](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_of_the_United_Kingdom "Nuclear weapons of the United Kingdom").[[75]](#cite_note-76)

The [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)")[[b]](#cite_note-77) is the government department charged with formulating and executing defence policy. It currently employs 56,860 civilian staff members as of 1 October 2015.[[76]](#cite_note-78) The department is administered by the secretary of state for defence who is assisted by the [Minister of State for the Armed Forces](/wiki/Minister_of_State_for_the_Armed_Forces "Minister of State for the Armed Forces"), [Minister for Defence Procurement](/wiki/Minister_of_State_for_Defence_Procurement_(UK) "Minister of State for Defence Procurement (UK)"), and Minister for Veterans' Affairs. Responsibility for the management of the forces is delegated to a number of committees: the [Defence Council](/wiki/Defence_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Defence Council of the United Kingdom"), [Chiefs of Staff Committee](/wiki/Chiefs_of_Staff_Committee "Chiefs of Staff Committee"), Defence Management Board and three single-service boards. The Defence Council, composed of senior representatives of the services and the Ministry of Defence, provides the "formal legal basis for the conduct of defence". The three constituent single-service committees ([Admiralty Board](/wiki/Admiralty_Board "Admiralty Board"), [Army Board](/wiki/Army_Board "Army Board") and [Air Force Board](/wiki/Air_Force_Board "Air Force Board")) are chaired by the secretary of state for defence.

The [chief of the defence staff](/wiki/Chief_of_the_Defence_Staff_(United_Kingdom) "Chief of the Defence Staff (United Kingdom)") (CDS) is the senior-most officer of the armed forces and is an appointment that can be held by an [admiral](/wiki/Admiral "Admiral"), [air chief marshal](/wiki/Air_chief_marshal "Air chief marshal") or [general](/wiki/General "General"). Before the practice was discontinued in the 1990s, those who were appointed to the position of CDS had been elevated to the [most senior rank](/wiki/Five-star_rank "Five-star rank") in their respective service.[[77]](#cite_note-79) The CDS, along with the permanent under secretary, are the principal military advisers to the secretary of state. All three services have their own respective professional chiefs; the [First Sea Lord](/wiki/First_Sea_Lord "First Sea Lord") for the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), the [chief of the general staff](/wiki/Chief_of_the_General_Staff_(United_Kingdom) "Chief of the General Staff (United Kingdom)") for the [Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") and the [chief of the air staff](/wiki/Chief_of_the_Air_Staff_(United_Kingdom) "Chief of the Air Staff (United Kingdom)") for the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force").

### Personnel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Personnel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Soldiers_Trooping_the_Colour%2C_16th_June_2007.jpg/250px-Soldiers_Trooping_the_Colour%2C_16th_June_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soldiers_Trooping_the_Colour,_16th_June_2007.jpg)

[Welsh Guards](/wiki/Welsh_Guards "Welsh Guards") [Trooping the Colour](/wiki/Trooping_the_Colour "Trooping the Colour")

As of 1 July 2025 the British Armed Forces are a professional force with a total strength of 180,779 personnel, consisting of 136,117 UK Regulars and 4,127 [Gurkhas](/wiki/Brigade_of_Gurkhas "Brigade of Gurkhas"), 31,967 [Volunteer Reserves](/wiki/Volunteer_Reserves_(United_Kingdom) "Volunteer Reserves (United Kingdom)") and 8,568 "Other Personnel".[[c]](#cite_note-81)[[78]](#cite_note-quarterly_personnel_report-80) As a percentage breakdown of UK Service Personnel, 77.1% are UK Regulars and Gurkhas, 18.8% are Volunteer Reserves and 4.1% are composed of Other Personnel.[[78]](#cite_note-quarterly_personnel_report-80) In addition, all ex-Regular personnel retain a "statutory liability for service" and are liable to be recalled (under Section 52 of the Reserve Forces Act (RFA) 1996) for duty during wartime, which is known as the [Regular Reserve](/wiki/Regular_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Regular Reserve (United Kingdom)"). MoD publications since April 2013 no longer report the entire strength of the Regular Reserve, instead they only give a figure for Regular Reserves who serve under a fixed-term reserve contract. These contracts are similar in nature to those of the Volunteer Reserve.[[79]](#cite_note-Regular_Reserve-82)

The distribution of personnel between the services and categories of service on 1 October 2025 was as follows:[[78]](#cite_note-quarterly_personnel_report-80)

| Service | Regular | Volunteer Reserve | Other personnel | Total |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Navy | 32,160 | 3,210 | 2,520 | **37,880** |
| Army and Gurkhas | 78,500 | 25,710 | 4,840 | **109,060** |
| Air Force | 30,670 | 3,020 | 1,430 | **35,130** |
| **Total** | **141,330** | **31,940** | **8,790** | **182,060** |

On 1 April 2024, most personnel in the UK Regular Forces were stationed in the United Kingdom (around 96%).[[80]](#cite_note-:3-83)

Of the 5,700 personnel stationed overseas, around two thirds were in Europe (66%), while 14% were stationed in North America, 6% in North Africa and the Middle East, 6% in Asia and 5% in Sub-Saharan Africa.[[80]](#cite_note-:3-83) 1,230 personnel were distributed across several regions in Germany, primarily [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia") as part of [British Army Germany](/wiki/British_Army_Germany "British Army Germany"). However, up to 750 of these were Locally Engaged Civilians.[[81]](#cite_note-84)
