[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Top_ten_military_expenditures_in_US%24_Bn._in_2014%2C_according_to_the_International_Institute_for_Strategic_Studies.PNG/250px-Top_ten_military_expenditures_in_US%24_Bn._in_2014%2C_according_to_the_International_Institute_for_Strategic_Studies.PNG)](/wiki/File:Top_ten_military_expenditures_in_US$_Bn._in_2014,_according_to_the_International_Institute_for_Strategic_Studies.PNG)

Top ten military expenditures in billion US$ in 2014

According to the [Stockholm International Peace Research Institute](/wiki/Stockholm_International_Peace_Research_Institute "Stockholm International Peace Research Institute"), the United Kingdom is in sixth place in the world's military spending list in 2024.[[82]](#cite_note-:2-85) For comparison: Great Britain spends more in absolute terms than Saudi Arabia, Ukraine, France or Japan, but less than India, Germany, Russia, China or the United States.[[82]](#cite_note-:2-85) In September 2011, according to Professor Malcolm Chalmers of the [Royal United Services Institute](/wiki/Royal_United_Services_Institute "Royal United Services Institute"), current "planned levels of defence spending should be enough for the United Kingdom to maintain its position as one of the world's top military powers, as well as being one of NATO-Europe's top military powers. Its edge – not least its qualitative edge – in relation to rising Asian powers seems set to erode, but will remain significant well into the 2020s, and possibly beyond."[[83]](#cite_note-86) The [Strategic Defence and Security Review 2015](/wiki/Strategic_Defence_and_Security_Review_2015 "Strategic Defence and Security Review 2015") committed to spending 2% of GDP on defence and announced a £178 billion investment over ten years in new equipment and capabilities.[[84]](#cite_note-87)[[85]](#cite_note-88) On 8 March 2023 Prime Minister [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak") announced a further £5bn in defence spending with a long-term goal of an increased spending to 2.5% of GDP.[[86]](#cite_note-89)

### Nuclear weapons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Nuclear weapons")]

Main article: [Nuclear weapons of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_of_the_United_Kingdom "Nuclear weapons of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Trident_II_missile_image.jpg/250px-Trident_II_missile_image.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trident_II_missile_image.jpg)

A [Trident II SLBM](/wiki/UGM-133_Trident_II "UGM-133 Trident II") being launched from a [*Vanguard*-class](/wiki/Vanguard-class_submarine "Vanguard-class submarine") submarine

The United Kingdom is one of five recognised nuclear weapon states under the [Non-Proliferation Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Non-Proliferation_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons") and maintains an independent [nuclear deterrent](/wiki/Deterrence_theory "Deterrence theory"), currently consisting of four [*Vanguard*-class](/wiki/Vanguard-class_submarine "Vanguard-class submarine") [ballistic missile submarines](/wiki/Ballistic_missile_submarine "Ballistic missile submarine"), [UGM-133 Trident II](/wiki/UGM-133_Trident_II "UGM-133 Trident II") [submarine-launched ballistic missiles](/wiki/Submarine-launched_ballistic_missile "Submarine-launched ballistic missile"), and 160 operational [thermonuclear warheads](/wiki/Thermonuclear_weapon "Thermonuclear weapon"). This is known as [Trident](/wiki/Trident_nuclear_programme "Trident nuclear programme") in both public and political discourse (with nomenclature taken after the UGM-133 Trident II ballistic missile). Trident is operated by the [Royal Navy Submarine Service](/wiki/Royal_Navy_Submarine_Service "Royal Navy Submarine Service"), charged with delivering a 'Continuous At-Sea Deterrent' (CASD) capability, whereby one of the *Vanguard*-class strategic submarines is always on patrol.[[87]](#cite_note-CASD-90) According to the British Government, since the introduction of [Polaris](/wiki/UK_Polaris_programme "UK Polaris programme") (Trident's predecessor) in the 1960s, from April 1969 "the Royal Navy's ballistic missile boats have not missed a single day on patrol",[[87]](#cite_note-CASD-90) giving what the [Defence Council](/wiki/Defence_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Defence Council of the United Kingdom") described in 1980 as a deterrent "effectively invulnerable to pre-emptive attack".[[88]](#cite_note-Future_Deterrent-91) As of 2015, it has been British Government policy for the *Vanguard*-class strategic submarines to carry no more than 40 nuclear warheads, delivered by eight UGM-133 Trident II ballistic missiles.[[89]](#cite_note-92) In contrast with the other recognised nuclear weapon states, the United Kingdom operates only a submarine-based delivery system, having decommissioned its tactical [WE.177](/wiki/WE.177 "WE.177") free-fall bombs in 1998.

The [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") voted on 18 July 2016 in favour of replacing the *Vanguard*-class submarines with a new generation of [*Dreadnought*-class submarines](/wiki/Dreadnought-class_submarine "Dreadnought-class submarine").[[90]](#cite_note-93) The programme will also contribute to extending the life of the UGM-133 Trident II ballistic missiles and modernise the infrastructure associated with the CASD.[[91]](#cite_note-WhitePaper-94)

Former [weapons of mass destruction possessed by the United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_and_weapons_of_mass_destruction "United Kingdom and weapons of mass destruction") include both biological and [chemical weapons](/wiki/Chemical_weapons_and_the_United_Kingdom "Chemical weapons and the United Kingdom"). These were renounced in 1956 and subsequently destroyed.

### Overseas military installations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Overseas military installations")]

Main article: [Overseas military bases of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Overseas_military_bases_of_the_United_Kingdom "Overseas military bases of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/United_Kingdom_overseas_military_installations_and_operations.png/250px-United_Kingdom_overseas_military_installations_and_operations.png)](/wiki/File:United_Kingdom_overseas_military_installations_and_operations.png)

British overseas military installations

Overseas military installations of the United Kingdom, and locally raised units of [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories").

Military interventions since 2000: [Palliser](/wiki/British_military_intervention_in_the_Sierra_Leone_Civil_War "British military intervention in the Sierra Leone Civil War") (Sierra Leone); [Herrick](/wiki/Operation_Herrick "Operation Herrick") (Afghanistan); [Enduring Freedom](/wiki/Operation_Enduring_Freedom_%E2%80%93_Horn_of_Africa "Operation Enduring Freedom – Horn of Africa") (Horn of Africa); [Telic](/wiki/Operation_Telic "Operation Telic") (Iraq); [Ellamy](/wiki/Operation_Ellamy "Operation Ellamy") (Libya); and [Shader](/wiki/Operation_Shader "Operation Shader") (Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant).

The British Armed Forces historically relied on four [Imperial fortress](/wiki/Imperial_fortress "Imperial fortress") colonies (Bermuda,[[92]](#cite_note-95) Gibraltar, Halifax and its environs in Nova Scotia, and Malta),[[93]](#cite_note-96) where dockyards were established, naval squadrons based, soldiers garrisoned,[[94]](#cite_note-97)[[95]](#cite_note-98)[[96]](#cite_note-99) and naval and military stores stockpiled.[[97]](#cite_note-100)[[98]](#cite_note-101)[[99]](#cite_note-102) These acted as lynchpins in maintaining British naval supremacy on the Atlantic and its connected seas.[[100]](#cite_note-103)[[101]](#cite_note-104)[[102]](#cite_note-105)[[103]](#cite_note-106)[[104]](#cite_note-107) As, until the end of the First World War, it was presumed the only navies that might prove a threat were all of countries on, or off, the Atlantic, no Imperial fortress was established in the Pacific or Indian Oceans, to which power would be extended from Bermuda and Malta following the completion of the Panama and Suez canals. Local-service military reserve units were raised in some of the Imperial fortresses (notably Bermuda and Malta), which could be embodied for full time service in war time to reinforce the regular garrisons, and these were funded by the War Office as part of the British Army. After the First World War, the growing belligerence and naval power of the Japanese Empire led to the construction of the Singapore Naval Base. The regular British Armed Forces otherwise were distributed around the world, were required to guard against invasion or rebellion, reinforced in some colonies by locally raised reserve forces. In colonies where there was no strategic requirement, regular forces were rarely stationed, with local governments encouraged to maintain and fund military reserve units as contributions to their own defence (although these units were ultimately under the control of the national, i.e. British, Government via the colonial Governors as defence is not a competency that has been delegated to local governments). Under the North Atlantic Treaty Organisation alliance, and with the steady reduction of both the British Empire and the British Armed Forces over the decades that followed the Second World War, the significance of the three remaining Imperial fortresses (military control of Halifax having passed to the new Dominion government following the 1867 [Confederation of Canada](/wiki/Confederation_of_Canada "Confederation of Canada"), and naval control transferred in 1905 to what was to become the [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy")) rapidly faded. The Bermuda-based [North America and West Indies Station](/wiki/North_America_and_West_Indies_Station "North America and West Indies Station") was abolished in 1956, and the last regular army units removed from the Bermuda Command in 1957 (leaving only two part-time reserve units), with the naval dockyard in Bermuda reduced to a base,[[105]](#cite_note-108) without repair or refit capabilities, in 1951 and finally closed in 1995, following the Cold War (United States and Canadian bases in Bermuda closed in the same period), leaving only the [Royal Bermuda Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Bermuda_Regiment "Royal Bermuda Regiment") and the [Bermuda Sea Cadet Corps](/wiki/Bermuda_Sea_Cadet_Corps "Bermuda Sea Cadet Corps") there today.[[106]](#cite_note-109) Malta became independent in 1964, and the last British armed forces personnel were removed from the former colony in 1979. Gibraltar continues to be used by the regular British Armed Forces, though the naval and military establishment in the colony (now termed a *British Overseas Territory*) has been reduced to several Royal Naval patrol craft, the locally raised [Royal Gibraltar Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Gibraltar_Regiment "Royal Gibraltar Regiment"), and a [Royal Air Force Station](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force_Station "Royal Air Force Station") without aircraft based on it.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Two_RAF_E-3D_Sentry_Aircraft_at_RAF_Akrotiri.jpg/250px-Two_RAF_E-3D_Sentry_Aircraft_at_RAF_Akrotiri.jpg)](/wiki/File:Two_RAF_E-3D_Sentry_Aircraft_at_RAF_Akrotiri.jpg)

Two [Boeing E-3 Sentry](/wiki/Boeing_E-3_Sentry "Boeing E-3 Sentry") aircraft at [RAF Akrotiri](/wiki/RAF_Akrotiri "RAF Akrotiri") in Cyprus

The British Armed Forces today maintain a number of overseas garrisons and military facilities which enable the country to conduct operations worldwide. The majority of Britain's permanent military installations are located on [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories") (BOTs) or [former colonies](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") which retain close diplomatic ties with the United Kingdom, and located in areas of strategic importance.[[15]](#cite_note-europarl-16) The most significant of these are the "Permanent Joint Operating Bases" (PJOBs), located on the four overseas territories of Cyprus ([British Forces Cyprus](/wiki/British_Forces_Cyprus "British Forces Cyprus")), Gibraltar ([British Forces Gibraltar](/wiki/British_Forces_Gibraltar "British Forces Gibraltar")), the Falkland Islands ([British Forces South Atlantic Islands](/wiki/Military_of_the_Falkland_Islands "Military of the Falkland Islands")) and Diego Garcia ([British Forces British Indian Ocean Territories](/wiki/British_Forces_British_Indian_Ocean_Territories "British Forces British Indian Ocean Territories")).[[107]](#cite_note-110) While not a PJOB, Ascension Island (another BOT) is home to the airbase [RAF Ascension Island](/wiki/RAF_Ascension_Island "RAF Ascension Island"), notable for use as a staging post during the 1982 [Falklands War](/wiki/Falklands_War "Falklands War"), the territory is also the site of a joint UK-US [signals intelligence](/wiki/Signals_intelligence "Signals intelligence") facility.[[15]](#cite_note-europarl-16)

Qatar is home to [RAF Al Udeid](/wiki/RAF_Al_Udeid "RAF Al Udeid"), a Royal Air Force outpost at [Al Udeid Air Base](/wiki/Al_Udeid_Air_Base "Al Udeid Air Base") which serves as the operational headquarters for [No. 83 Expeditionary Air Group](/wiki/No._83_Expeditionary_Air_Group_(United_Kingdom) "No. 83 Expeditionary Air Group (United Kingdom)") and its operations across the Middle East.[[108]](#cite_note-111) A large Royal Navy [Naval Support Facility](/wiki/HMS_Jufair "HMS Jufair") (NSF) is located in Bahrain, established in 2016 it marks the British return East of Suez.[[109]](#cite_note-112) In support of the [Five Power Defence Arrangements](/wiki/Five_Power_Defence_Arrangements "Five Power Defence Arrangements") (FPDA), the United Kingdom retains a naval repair and logistics support facility at [Sembawang wharf](/wiki/Sembawang "Sembawang"), Singapore.[[15]](#cite_note-europarl-16)[[110]](#cite_note-NP1022-113) Other overseas military installations include; [British Forces Brunei](/wiki/British_Forces_Brunei "British Forces Brunei"),[[111]](#cite_note-Brunei-114) [British Army Germany](/wiki/British_Army_Germany "British Army Germany"),[[112]](#cite_note-Germany-115) the [British Army Training Unit Kenya](/wiki/British_Army_Training_Unit_Kenya "British Army Training Unit Kenya"),[[113]](#cite_note-Africa-116) [British Army Training Unit Suffield](/wiki/British_Army_Training_Unit_Suffield "British Army Training Unit Suffield") in Canada,[[114]](#cite_note-Canada-117) [British Army Training and Support Unit Belize](/wiki/British_Army_Training_and_Support_Unit_Belize "British Army Training and Support Unit Belize"), and [British Gurkhas Nepal](/wiki/British_Gurkhas_Nepal "British Gurkhas Nepal").[[115]](#cite_note-Nepal-118)

Some British Overseas Territories also maintain locally raised units and regiments; [The Royal Bermuda Regiment](/wiki/The_Royal_Bermuda_Regiment "The Royal Bermuda Regiment"), the [Falkland Islands Defence Force](/wiki/Falkland_Islands_Defence_Force "Falkland Islands Defence Force"), the [Royal Gibraltar Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Gibraltar_Regiment "Royal Gibraltar Regiment"), the [Royal Montserrat Defence Force](/wiki/Royal_Montserrat_Defence_Force "Royal Montserrat Defence Force"), the [Cayman Islands Regiment](/wiki/Cayman_Islands_Regiment "Cayman Islands Regiment"), and the [Turks and Caicos Regiment](/wiki/Turks_and_Caicos_Regiment "Turks and Caicos Regiment"). Though their primary mission is "home defence", individuals have volunteered for operational duties. The Royal Bermuda Regiment is an amalgam of the [Bermuda Militia Artillery](/wiki/Bermuda_Militia_Artillery "Bermuda Militia Artillery") (which had been part of the [Royal Regiment of Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Regiment_of_Artillery "Royal Regiment of Artillery")) and the [Bermuda Volunteer Rifle Corps](/wiki/Bermuda_Volunteer_Rifle_Corps "Bermuda Volunteer Rifle Corps"),[[116]](#cite_note-119) raised in the 1890s as Imperial forces funded by the War Office as part of the British Army,[[117]](#cite_note-120) and both antecedent units sent contingents to the Western Front during the First World War. They also sent contingents that served in North-Western Europe, and Italy and North Africa during the Second World War. The Royal Gibraltar Regiment mobilised section-sized units for attachment to British regiments deployed during the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War").[[118]](#cite_note-121)[[119]](#cite_note-122) The [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man"), a [Crown dependency](/wiki/Crown_dependency "Crown dependency") hosts a multi-capability recruiting and training unit of the [British Army Reserve](/wiki/Army_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Army Reserve (United Kingdom)").[[120]](#cite_note-Isle_of_Man_Army_Reserve-123)

Since 1969 Britain has had a military satellite communications system, [Skynet](/wiki/Skynet_(satellite) "Skynet (satellite)"), initially in large part to support [East of Suez](/wiki/East_of_Suez "East of Suez") bases and deployments. Since 2015 Skynet has offered near global coverage.[[121]](#cite_note-defenceconnect-20160516-124)

### Expeditionary forces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Expeditionary forces")]

The British Armed Forces place significant importance in the ability to conduct [expeditionary warfare](/wiki/Expeditionary_warfare "Expeditionary warfare").[[69]](#cite_note-Adrian-70) While the armed forces are expeditionary in nature, it maintains a core of "high readiness" forces trained and equipped to deploy at very short notice, these include; the [Joint Expeditionary Force (Maritime)](/wiki/Joint_Expeditionary_Force_(Maritime) "Joint Expeditionary Force (Maritime)") (Royal Navy), [UK Commando Force](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Commando_Force "United Kingdom Commando Force") (Royal Marines), and [16 Air Assault Brigade](/wiki/16_Air_Assault_Brigade "16 Air Assault Brigade") (British Army). Frequently, these forces will act as part of a larger tri-service effort, under the direction of [Permanent Joint Headquarters](/wiki/Permanent_Joint_Headquarters "Permanent Joint Headquarters"), or along with like-minded allies under the [Joint Expeditionary Force](/wiki/Joint_Expeditionary_Force "Joint Expeditionary Force"). Similarly, under the auspices of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), such expeditionary forces are designed to meet Britain's obligations to the [Allied Rapid Reaction Corps](/wiki/Allied_Rapid_Reaction_Corps "Allied Rapid Reaction Corps") and other NATO operations.

In 2010, the governments of the United Kingdom and [France](/wiki/France "France") signed the [Lancaster House Treaties](/wiki/The_Lancaster_House_Treaties_(2010) "The Lancaster House Treaties (2010)") which committed both governments to the creation of a Franco-British [Combined Joint Expeditionary Force](/wiki/Combined_Joint_Expeditionary_Force "Combined Joint Expeditionary Force").[[122]](#cite_note-125) It is envisaged as a deployable joint force, for use in a wide range of crisis scenarios, up to and including high intensity combat operations. As a joint force it involves all three armed Services: a land component composed of formations at national brigade level, maritime and air components with their associated Headquarters, together with logistics and support functions.[[123]](#cite_note-126)

## Branches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Branches")]

### Royal Navy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Royal Navy")]

Main article: [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_%28R08%29_underway_during_trials_with_HMS_Sutherland_%28F81%29_and_HMS_Iron_Duke_%28F234%29_on_28_June_2017_%2845162784%29.jpg/250px-HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_%28R08%29_underway_during_trials_with_HMS_Sutherland_%28F81%29_and_HMS_Iron_Duke_%28F234%29_on_28_June_2017_%2845162784%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_(R08)_underway_during_trials_with_HMS_Sutherland_(F81)_and_HMS_Iron_Duke_(F234)_on_28_June_2017_(45162784).jpg)

[HMS *Queen Elizabeth*](/wiki/HMS_Queen_Elizabeth_(R08) "HMS Queen Elizabeth (R08)"), a [*Queen Elizabeth*-class](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth-class_aircraft_carrier "Queen Elizabeth-class aircraft carrier") [supercarrier](/wiki/Supercarrier "Supercarrier") on sea trials in June 2017

The Royal Navy is a technologically sophisticated naval force,[[124]](#cite_note-127) and as of February 2026 consists of [63 commissioned ships](/wiki/List_of_active_Royal_Navy_ships "List of active Royal Navy ships") with an additional 9 support vessels of various types operated by the [Royal Fleet Auxiliary](/wiki/Royal_Fleet_Auxiliary "Royal Fleet Auxiliary"). Command of deployable assets is exercised by the [Fleet Commander](/wiki/Fleet_Commander "Fleet Commander") of the [Naval Service](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Naval_Service "His Majesty's Naval Service").[[125]](#cite_note-128) Personnel matters are the responsibility of the [Second Sea Lord](/wiki/Second_Sea_Lord "Second Sea Lord")/Commander-in-Chief Naval Home Command, an appointment usually held by a vice-admiral.[[126]](#cite_note-129)

The Surface Fleet consists of [aircraft carriers](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier"), [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer"), [frigates](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate"), [patrol vessels](/wiki/Patrol_boat "Patrol boat"), [mine-countermeasure vessels](/wiki/Minesweeper "Minesweeper"), and other miscellaneous vessels. The Surface Fleet has been structured around a single fleet since the abolition of the [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Fleet "Eastern Fleet") and [Western](/wiki/Western_Fleet_(United_Kingdom) "Western Fleet (United Kingdom)") fleets in 1971.[[127]](#cite_note-130) The recently built [Type 45 destroyers](/wiki/Type_45_destroyer "Type 45 destroyer") are stealthy and technologically advanced air-defence destroyers. The Royal Navy has commissioned two [*Queen Elizabeth*-class](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth-class_aircraft_carrier "Queen Elizabeth-class aircraft carrier") aircraft carriers, embarking an air-group including the advanced fifth-generation multi-role fighter, the [F-35B Lightning](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin_F-35_Lightning_II "Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II").[[128]](#cite_note-131)

A submarine service has existed within the Royal Navy for more than 100 years. The [Submarine Service](/wiki/Royal_Navy_Submarine_Service "Royal Navy Submarine Service")'s four [*Vanguard*-class](/wiki/Vanguard-class_submarine "Vanguard-class submarine") nuclear-powered submarines carry [Trident II ballistic missiles](/wiki/UGM-133_Trident_II "UGM-133 Trident II"), forming the [United Kingdom's nuclear deterrent](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_of_the_United_Kingdom "Nuclear weapons of the United Kingdom"). Seven [*Astute*-class](/wiki/Astute-class_submarine "Astute-class submarine") nuclear-powered fleet (attack) submarines have been ordered, with six completed and one under construction. The *Astute* class are the most advanced and largest fleet submarines ever built for the Royal Navy and will maintain Britain's nuclear-powered submarine fleet capabilities for decades to come.

#### Royal Marines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Royal Marines")]

Main article: [Royal Marines](/wiki/Royal_Marines "Royal Marines")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/FOB_Jackson_2009.jpg/250px-FOB_Jackson_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:FOB_Jackson_2009.jpg)

Royal Marines of [45 Commando](/wiki/45_Commando "45 Commando") at FOB Jackson, Helmand Province, 2009

The Royal Marines are the Royal Navy's amphibious troops. Consisting of a single manoeuvre brigade (UK Commando Force) and various independent units, the Royal Marines specialise in [amphibious](/wiki/Amphibious_warfare "Amphibious warfare"), [arctic](/wiki/Arctic_warfare "Arctic warfare"), and [mountain warfare](/wiki/Mountain_warfare "Mountain warfare").[[129]](#cite_note-132) Contained within UK Commando Force are three attached army units; 383 Commando Petroleum Troop RLC, [29th Commando Regiment Royal Artillery](/wiki/29th_Commando_Regiment_Royal_Artillery "29th Commando Regiment Royal Artillery"), a field artillery regiment based in Plymouth, and [24 Commando Regiment Royal Engineers](/wiki/24_Commando_Regiment_Royal_Engineers "24 Commando Regiment Royal Engineers").[[130]](#cite_note-133) The [Commando Logistic Regiment](/wiki/Commando_Logistic_Regiment "Commando Logistic Regiment") consists of personnel from the Army, Royal Marines, and Royal Navy.[[131]](#cite_note-134)

### British Army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: British Army")]

Main article: [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army")

The British Army is the land force of the British Armed Forces, and is made up of the Regular Army and the part-time [Army Reserve](/wiki/Army_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Army Reserve (United Kingdom)"). The Army is commanded by the [Chief of the General Staff](/wiki/Chief_of_the_General_Staff_(United_Kingdom) "Chief of the General Staff (United Kingdom)"), a [four-star general](/wiki/Four-star_general "Four-star general") within [Army Headquarters](/wiki/Army_Headquarters_(United_Kingdom) "Army Headquarters (United Kingdom)"), based at [Andover](/wiki/Marlborough_Lines "Marlborough Lines").[[132]](#cite_note-135)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/ILauncher_%28within_Sky_Sabre%29.jpg/250px-ILauncher_%28within_Sky_Sabre%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:ILauncher_(within_Sky_Sabre).jpg)

[Sky Sabre](/wiki/Sky_Sabre "Sky Sabre") (with *Land Ceptor* missiles) ground-based air defence system as operated by [7th Air Defence Group](/wiki/7th_Air_Defence_Group "7th Air Defence Group").

Deployable combat formations are;[[133]](#cite_note-136)[[134]](#cite_note-:1-137)

* [1st (UK) Division](/wiki/1st_(United_Kingdom)_Division "1st (United Kingdom) Division"), consisting of [16 Air Assault Brigade Combat Team](/wiki/16_Air_Assault_Brigade_Combat_Team "16 Air Assault Brigade Combat Team") and four other Light or Light Mechanised Brigade Combat Teams, with supporting engineering, logistic, intelligence and signals units.[[135]](#cite_note-:0-138)
* [3rd (UK) Division](/wiki/3rd_(United_Kingdom)_Division "3rd (United Kingdom) Division"), consisting of [3rd Deep Reconnaissance Strike Brigade](/wiki/3rd_Deep_Reconnaissance_Strike_Brigade "3rd Deep Reconnaissance Strike Brigade"), [7 Air Defence Group](/wiki/7th_Air_Defence_Group "7th Air Defence Group"), and two Armoured Brigade Combat Teams, with supporting engineering, logistic, intelligence and signals units.[[135]](#cite_note-:0-138)
* [Field Army Troops](/wiki/Field_Army_Troops "Field Army Troops"), consisting of the new [Ranger Regiment](/wiki/Ranger_Regiment_(United_Kingdom) "Ranger Regiment (United Kingdom)"), in [Army Special Operations Brigade](/wiki/Army_Special_Operations_Brigade "Army Special Operations Brigade"); Security Force Assistance Brigade and [77 Brigade](/wiki/77th_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "77th Brigade (United Kingdom)"), a [psychological operations](/wiki/Psychological_warfare "Psychological warfare") unit.[[135]](#cite_note-:0-138)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Boxer_MIV_on_2019_Army_Combat_Power_Demonstration_%28ACPD%29_-_9.jpg/250px-Boxer_MIV_on_2019_Army_Combat_Power_Demonstration_%28ACPD%29_-_9.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boxer_MIV_on_2019_Army_Combat_Power_Demonstration_(ACPD)_-_9.jpg)

[Boxer](/wiki/Boxer_(armoured_fighting_vehicle) "Boxer (armoured fighting vehicle)") [mechanised infantry vehicle](/wiki/Mechanised_Infantry_Vehicle "Mechanised Infantry Vehicle") (MIV) with [remote weapon station](/wiki/Remote_weapon_station "Remote weapon station") (RWS).

The [Infantry of the British Army](/wiki/Infantry_of_the_British_Army "Infantry of the British Army") has a strength of 48 battalions (32 regular and 16 reserve), structured under 17 unique regiments.[[136]](#cite_note-139) These battalions are trained and equipped for specific roles within their respective Brigade Combat Teams (BCT); [Light Infantry](/wiki/Light_infantry "Light infantry"), such as the famous 1st Battalion [Grenadier Guards](/wiki/Grenadier_Guards "Grenadier Guards"), within the [4th Light Brigade Combat Team](/wiki/4th_Light_Brigade_Combat_Team "4th Light Brigade Combat Team"), fight on foot without armoured vehicles; [Light Mechanised Infantry](/wiki/Mechanized_infantry "Mechanized infantry"), such as the 1st Battalion [Royal Yorkshire Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Yorkshire_Regiment "Royal Yorkshire Regiment"), within the [7th Light Mechanised Brigade Combat Team](/wiki/7th_Light_Mechanised_Brigade_Combat_Team "7th Light Mechanised Brigade Combat Team"), operate the [Foxhound](/wiki/Ocelot_(vehicle) "Ocelot (vehicle)") protected mobility vehicle; Armoured Infantry (to become Heavy Mechanised Infantry under [Future Soldier](/wiki/Future_Soldier_(British_Army) "Future Soldier (British Army)")), such as the 1st Battalion [Royal Regiment of Fusiliers](/wiki/Royal_Regiment_of_Fusiliers "Royal Regiment of Fusiliers"), within the [20th Armoured Infantry Brigade Combat Team](/wiki/20th_Armoured_Brigade_Combat_Team_(United_Kingdom) "20th Armoured Brigade Combat Team (United Kingdom)"), operate the [Warrior](/wiki/Warrior_tracked_armoured_vehicle "Warrior tracked armoured vehicle") infantry fighting vehicle (IFV), but will be equipped with the new [Boxer](/wiki/Boxer_(armoured_fighting_vehicle) "Boxer (armoured fighting vehicle)") mechanised infantry vehicle from 2024.[[137]](#cite_note-140)[[134]](#cite_note-:1-137)[[138]](#cite_note-141)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/B_Company_2_Battalion_the_Parachute_Regiment_MOD_45167489.jpg/250px-B_Company_2_Battalion_the_Parachute_Regiment_MOD_45167489.jpg)](/wiki/File:B_Company_2_Battalion_the_Parachute_Regiment_MOD_45167489.jpg)

[2PARA](/wiki/2nd_Battalion,_Parachute_Regiment "2nd Battalion, Parachute Regiment") on a [live fire exercise](/wiki/Live_fire_exercise "Live fire exercise") operating the [Javelin anti-tank guided missile](/wiki/Javelin_ATGM "Javelin ATGM"), [GPMG](/wiki/FN_MAG "FN MAG"), and [GMG](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_GMG "Heckler & Koch GMG") from a [RWMIK](/wiki/Land_Rover_Wolf "Land Rover Wolf").

The four battalions of the [Parachute Regiment](/wiki/Parachute_Regiment_(United_Kingdom) "Parachute Regiment (United Kingdom)"), forming [16 Air Assault Brigade Combat Team](/wiki/16_Air_Assault_Brigade_Combat_Team "16 Air Assault Brigade Combat Team") and part of [Special Forces Support Group](/wiki/Special_Forces_Support_Group "Special Forces Support Group"), are the British Army's elite airborne infanteers, held at high readiness and specialising in rapid deployment by parachute and helicopter, widely regarded as the "fittest, most aggressive, resilient and disciplined regiment in the British Army."[[139]](#cite_note-142)[[140]](#cite_note-143)[[141]](#cite_note-144)[[142]](#cite_note-145)

The [Royal Armoured Corps](/wiki/Royal_Armoured_Corps "Royal Armoured Corps") provides the armoured capability of the British Army. The [Royal Tank Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Tank_Regiment "Royal Tank Regiment"), [Queen's Royal Hussars](/wiki/Queen%27s_Royal_Hussars "Queen's Royal Hussars") and [Royal Wessex Yeomanry](/wiki/Royal_Wessex_Yeomanry "Royal Wessex Yeomanry") (of the [Army Reserve](/wiki/Army_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Army Reserve (United Kingdom)")) operate [Challenger 2](/wiki/Challenger_2 "Challenger 2") main battle tanks, which are being upgraded to [Challenger 3](/wiki/Challenger_3 "Challenger 3"), and are part of [3rd (UK) Division](/wiki/3rd_(United_Kingdom)_Division "3rd (United Kingdom) Division")'s Armoured Brigade Combat Teams. Armoured Cavalry regiments, such as the [Royal Dragoon Guards](/wiki/Royal_Dragoon_Guards "Royal Dragoon Guards"), currently operate the [Warrior IFV](/wiki/Warrior_tracked_armoured_vehicle "Warrior tracked armoured vehicle") on an interim basis, until [Ajax](/wiki/General_Dynamics_Ajax "General Dynamics Ajax") reaches [full operating capability](/wiki/Full_operating_capability "Full operating capability"). There are six Light Cavalry regiments (three Regular + three Reserve) equipped with the [Jackal 2](/wiki/Jackal_(vehicle) "Jackal (vehicle)") and [Coyote TSV](/wiki/Jackal_(vehicle)#Coyote "Jackal (vehicle)"), tasked with providing reconnaissance and fire support. The [Household Cavalry](/wiki/Household_Cavalry "Household Cavalry"), made up of the [Life Guards](/wiki/Life_Guards_(United_Kingdom) "Life Guards (United Kingdom)") and the [Blues and Royals](/wiki/Blues_and_Royals "Blues and Royals"), operate in a dual role of Armoured Cavalry and Mounted Ceremonial on [Horse Guards](/wiki/Horse_Guards_(building) "Horse Guards (building)") in London, and for state occasions.[[143]](#cite_note-146)[[144]](#cite_note-147)[[145]](#cite_note-148)[[134]](#cite_note-:1-137)[[146]](#cite_note-149)

### Royal Air Force

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Royal Air Force")]

Main article: [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Royal_Air_Force_Eurofighter_EF-2000_Typhoon_F2_Lofting-1.jpg/250px-Royal_Air_Force_Eurofighter_EF-2000_Typhoon_F2_Lofting-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Air_Force_Eurofighter_EF-2000_Typhoon_F2_Lofting-1.jpg)

The [Eurofighter Typhoon](/wiki/Eurofighter_Typhoon "Eurofighter Typhoon") [multirole combat aircraft](/wiki/Multirole_combat_aircraft "Multirole combat aircraft")

The Royal Air Force has a large operational fleet that fulfils various roles, consisting of both [fixed-wing and rotary aircraft](/wiki/List_of_active_United_Kingdom_military_aircraft "List of active United Kingdom military aircraft").[[147]](#cite_note-150) Frontline aircraft are controlled by [Air Command](/wiki/RAF_Air_Command "RAF Air Command"), which is organised into five [groups](/wiki/Group_(air_force) "Group (air force)") defined by function: [1 Group](/wiki/No._1_Group_RAF "No. 1 Group RAF") (Air Combat), [2 Group](/wiki/No._2_Group_RAF "No. 2 Group RAF") (Air Support), [11 Group](/wiki/No._11_Group_RAF "No. 11 Group RAF") (Air and Space operations),[[148]](#cite_note-RAF-151) [22 Group](/wiki/No._22_Group "No. 22 Group") (training aircraft and ground facilities) and [38 Group](/wiki/No._38_Group_RAF "No. 38 Group RAF") (Royal Air Force's Engineering, Logistics, Communications and Medical Operations units).[[148]](#cite_note-RAF-151) In addition [83 Expeditionary Air Group](/wiki/No._83_Expeditionary_Air_Group_(United_Kingdom) "No. 83 Expeditionary Air Group (United Kingdom)") directs formations in the Middle East and the [38 Group](/wiki/No._38_Group_RAF "No. 38 Group RAF") combines the expeditionary [combat support](/wiki/Combat_Support "Combat Support") and [combat service support](/wiki/Combat_Service_Support "Combat Service Support") units of the RAF. Deployable formations consist of Expeditionary Air [Wings](/wiki/Wing_(air_force_unit) "Wing (air force unit)") and [squadrons](/wiki/Squadron_(aviation) "Squadron (aviation)")—the basic unit of the Air Force.[[149]](#cite_note-152)[[150]](#cite_note-153) Independent [flights](/wiki/Flight_(military_unit) "Flight (military unit)") are deployed to facilities in Brunei, the Falkland Islands, Iraq, and the United States.[[151]](#cite_note-154)

The Royal Air Force operates multi-role and single-role fighters, reconnaissance and patrol aircraft, tankers, transports, helicopters, unmanned aerial vehicles, and various types of training aircraft.[[152]](#cite_note-155)

Ground units are also maintained by the Royal Air Force, most prominently the [RAF Police](/wiki/RAF_Police "RAF Police") and the [Royal Air Force Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force_Regiment "Royal Air Force Regiment") (RAF Regt). The Royal Air Force Regiment essentially functions as the ground defence force of the RAF, optimised for the specialist role of fighting on and around forward airfields, which are densely packed with operationally vital aircraft, equipment, infrastructure and personnel.[[153]](#cite_note-156) The Regiment contains nine regular squadrons, supported by five squadrons of the [Royal Auxiliary Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Auxiliary_Air_Force "Royal Auxiliary Air Force") Regiment. In addition, it provides [Forward Air Controllers](/wiki/Forward_air_control "Forward air control") to defence as well as a contribution to the [Special Forces Support Group](/wiki/Special_Forces_Support_Group "Special Forces Support Group").[[154]](#cite_note-157)[[155]](#cite_note-158)

## Ministry of Defence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Ministry of Defence")]

Main article: [Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/RFA_Argus%2C_FS_Meuse_and_RS_Regina_Maria_at_anchor_in_the_Solent%2C_in_readiness_for_the_International_Fleet_Review._MOD_45145024.jpg/250px-RFA_Argus%2C_FS_Meuse_and_RS_Regina_Maria_at_anchor_in_the_Solent%2C_in_readiness_for_the_International_Fleet_Review._MOD_45145024.jpg)](/wiki/File:RFA_Argus,_FS_Meuse_and_RS_Regina_Maria_at_anchor_in_the_Solent,_in_readiness_for_the_International_Fleet_Review._MOD_45145024.jpg)

[RFA *Argus*](/wiki/RFA_Argus "RFA Argus") (left), the fleet's [aviation training](/wiki/Flight_training "Flight training") and [hospital ship](/wiki/Hospital_ship "Hospital ship")

The Ministry of Defence maintains a number of civilian agencies in support of the British Armed Forces. Although they are civilian, they play a vital role in supporting Armed Forces operations, and in certain circumstances are under military discipline:

* The **[Royal Fleet Auxiliary](/wiki/Royal_Fleet_Auxiliary "Royal Fleet Auxiliary")** (RFA) operates 9 ships which primarily serve to replenish Royal Navy warships at sea, and also provides an amphibious warfare capability through its three [Bay-class](/wiki/Bay-class_landing_ship_dock "Bay-class landing ship dock") [landing ship dock](/wiki/Landing_ship_dock "Landing ship dock") vessels. It is manned by 1,750 civilian personnel and is funded and run by the Ministry of Defence.
* The **[Ministry of Defence Police](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_Police "Ministry of Defence Police")** (MDP) has an established strength of 2,700 police officers which provide armed security, counter terrorism, uniformed policing and investigative services to Ministry of Defence property, personnel, and installations throughout the United Kingdom.
* The **[Defence Equipment and Support](/wiki/Defence_Equipment_and_Support "Defence Equipment and Support")** (DE&S) is the merged procurement and support organisation within the UK [Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)"). It came into being on 2 April 2007, bringing together the MoD's [Defence Procurement Agency](/wiki/Defence_Procurement_Agency "Defence Procurement Agency") and the [Defence Logistics Organisation](/wiki/Defence_Logistics_Organisation "Defence Logistics Organisation") under the leadership of General Sir [Kevin O'Donoghue](/wiki/Kevin_O%27Donoghue "Kevin O'Donoghue") as the first [Chief of Defence Materiel](/wiki/Chief_of_Defence_Materiel "Chief of Defence Materiel"). As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit) it has a civilian and military workforce of approx. 20,000 personnel. DE&S is overseen by the [Minister for Defence Equipment, Support and Technology](/wiki/Minister_for_Defence_Equipment,_Support_and_Technology "Minister for Defence Equipment, Support and Technology").
* The **[UK Hydrographic Office](/wiki/UK_Hydrographic_Office "UK Hydrographic Office")** (UKHO) is an organisation within the [UK government](/wiki/UK_government "UK government") responsible for providing [navigational](/wiki/Navigational "Navigational") and other hydrographic information for national, civil and defence requirements. The UKHO is located in [Taunton](/wiki/Taunton "Taunton"), [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset "Somerset"), on Admiralty Way and has a workforce of approximately 1,000 staff.

## Recruitment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Recruitment")]

Further information: [Recruitment in the British Army](/wiki/Recruitment_in_the_British_Army "Recruitment in the British Army")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Royal_Marine_During_Passing_Out_Parade_MOD_45154046.jpg/250px-Royal_Marine_During_Passing_Out_Parade_MOD_45154046.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Marine_During_Passing_Out_Parade_MOD_45154046.jpg)

A newly qualified [Royal Marine](/wiki/Royal_Marine "Royal Marine") of 122 Troop Kings Squad is pictured during a [passing out parade](/wiki/Passing_out_(military) "Passing out (military)") in 2011, having undergone the recruitment process and selection and training

All three services of the British Armed Forces recruit primarily from within the United Kingdom, although citizens from the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") are equally eligible to join.[[156]](#cite_note-Times-159) The minimum recruitment age is 16 years (although personnel may not serve on armed operations below 18 years, and if under 18 must also have parental consent to join); the maximum recruitment age depends whether the application is for a regular or reserve role; there are further variations in age limit for different corps/regiments. The normal term of engagement is 22 years; however, the minimum service required before resignation is 4 years, plus, in the case of the Army, any service person below the age of 18. A note to add is that in the United Kingdom, people may join the "Cadet Forces" such as the army cadets, Royal Air Force Air Cadets or the sea and Royal Marine Cadets. Young people may join these organisations which are either funded or affiliated with the MOD from the age of 13-18, there is no obligation to then join the armed forces however it teaches key skills in both civilian and military life and is a key recruitment drive for the armed forces.[[157]](#cite_note-160) At present, the yearly intake into the armed forces is 11,880 (per the 12 months to 31 March 2014).[[158]](#cite_note-PersonnelGOV-161)

Excluding the [Brigade of Gurkhas](/wiki/Brigade_of_Gurkhas "Brigade of Gurkhas") and the [Royal Irish Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Irish_Regiment_(1992) "Royal Irish Regiment (1992)"), as of 1 April 2014 there are approximately 11,200 Black and Minority Ethnic (BME) persons serving as Regulars across the three service branches; of those, 6,610 were recruited from outside the United Kingdom. In total, Black and Minority Ethnic persons represent 7.1% of all service personnel, an increase from 6.6% in 2010.[[158]](#cite_note-PersonnelGOV-161)

Since the year 2000, [sexual orientation has not been a factor considered in recruitment](/wiki/Sexual_orientation_and_the_military_of_the_United_Kingdom "Sexual orientation and the military of the United Kingdom"), and homosexuals can serve openly in the armed forces. All branches of the forces have actively recruited at [Gay Pride](/wiki/Gay_Pride "Gay Pride") events.[[159]](#cite_note-162)[[160]](#cite_note-163) The forces keep no formal figures concerning the number of gay and lesbian serving soldiers, saying that the sexual orientation of personnel is considered irrelevant and not monitored.[[161]](#cite_note-164)

### Role of women

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Role of women")]

Further information: [Women in the military by country § United Kingdom](/wiki/Women_in_the_military_by_country#United_Kingdom "Women in the military by country")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Hrh_Princess_Elizabeth_Undergoing_Instruction_at_the_Auxiliary_Territorial_Service_Training_Centre%2C_April_1945_H41676.jpg/250px-Hrh_Princess_Elizabeth_Undergoing_Instruction_at_the_Auxiliary_Territorial_Service_Training_Centre%2C_April_1945_H41676.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hrh_Princess_Elizabeth_Undergoing_Instruction_at_the_Auxiliary_Territorial_Service_Training_Centre,_April_1945_H41676.jpg)

[Princess Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") (later Queen Elizabeth II) in the [Auxiliary Territorial Service](/wiki/Auxiliary_Territorial_Service "Auxiliary Territorial Service"), April 1945

[Women](/wiki/Women "Women") have been part of the armed forces, on and off, for centuries, more fully integrated since the early 1990s, including flying fast jets and commanding warships or artillery batteries. As of 1 April 2014, there were approximately 15,840 women serving in the armed forces, representing 9.9% of all service personnel.[[158]](#cite_note-PersonnelGOV-161) The first female military pilot was [Flight Lieutenant](/wiki/Flight_Lieutenant "Flight Lieutenant") [Julie Ann Gibson](/wiki/Julie_Ann_Gibson "Julie Ann Gibson") while Flight Lieutenant [Jo Salter](/wiki/Jo_Salter "Jo Salter") was the first fast-jet pilot, the latter flying a Tornado GR1 on missions patrolling the then [Northern Iraqi No-Fly Zone](/wiki/Iraqi_no-fly_zones "Iraqi no-fly zones").[[162]](#cite_note-165) Flight Lieutenant Juliette Fleming and [Squadron Leader](/wiki/Squadron_Leader "Squadron Leader") Nikki Thomas recently[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] were the first Tornado GR4 crew.[[163]](#cite_note-166) While enforcing the Libyan No-Fly Zone, Flight Lieutenant Helen Seymour was identified as the first female Eurofighter Typhoon pilot.[[164]](#cite_note-167)

In August 2011, it was announced that a female lieutenant commander, [Sarah West](/wiki/Sarah_West "Sarah West"), was to command the [frigate](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate") [HMS *Portland*](/wiki/HMS_Portland_(F79) "HMS Portland (F79)").[[165]](#cite_note-168) In July 2016, it was announced that women would be allowed to serve in close combat, starting with the Royal Armoured Corps.[[166]](#cite_note-169) In July 2017, the Secretary of Defence announced that women would be allowed to enlist in the [RAF Regiment](/wiki/RAF_Regiment "RAF Regiment") from September 2017, a year ahead of schedule.[[167]](#cite_note-170) In 2018, women were allowed to apply for all roles in the British military, including the [special forces](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Special_Forces "United Kingdom Special Forces").[[168]](#cite_note-171) As of 10 June 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=British_Armed_Forces&action=edit), the [most senior serving woman](/wiki/List_of_senior_female_officers_of_the_British_Armed_Forces "List of senior female officers of the British Armed Forces") is [four-star](/wiki/Four_star_rank "Four star rank") General [Dame Sharon Nesmith](/wiki/Sharon_Nesmith "Sharon Nesmith").
